Party_Details.Name: Sean Colville Niven
Party_Details.Facts: Sean Colville Niven, the second defendant, has been involved in marketing and selling Australian properties since 1989 and had extensive experience and connections with Australian property developers. Prior to December 2008, he worked with the third defendant in JL Property Group Pte Ltd, a company selling Australian property. In December 2008, he and the third defendant decided to start their own company to sell Australian properties and resigned from JL Property. They approached Goh Cheng Liang for a $900,000 loan as start-up capital for the new venture, which became APG. As a condition of the loan, Mr Goh required his nominees to become directors and shareholders of APG. On incorporation, APG allotted 360,000 ordinary shares to the second defendant, 160,000 to the third defendant, and shares to the non-executive directors, although the shares were issued as fully paid-up without actual payment. The second defendant was responsible for securing marketing rights from Australian developers and managing APG’s finances. After the non-executive directors resigned in November 2009 and transferred their shares to him, his shareholding increased to 640,000 shares. On 8 December 2009, Ms Lim was appointed a director, and on 22 January 2010 the second defendant sold and transferred 240,000 shares to her for $160,000, with the transfer form stating the shares were fully paid up. APG’s accounts were disorganised, and Firstwaters Consultants Pte Ltd was engaged to reconstruct them. Ferrier Hodgson Pte Ltd was later appointed to review APG’s accounts and, in a draft summary sent on 11 October 2010, noted that many payments, including to the second and third defendants, lacked supporting vouchers, that there was no clear indication of benefits and entitlements, that professional fees had been paid without relevance to APG, that payments had been made to the second defendant in Australia by internet withdrawals and to an Australian company, and that APG had paid for the second defendant’s personal expenses. On 2 July 2010, chartered accountant John Kennedy Stuart advised that APG was technically insolvent. On 6 September 2010, the second defendant, third defendant, Ms Lim and APG executed the September Agreement under which Ms Lim agreed to capitalise $175,000 owed to her and inject $150,000 for 325,000 new shares, and the second defendant acknowledged owing APG $300,450.37, with 40% to be written off and the balance to be repaid in instalments by 31 December 2011 and 31 December 2012. The Agreement also allocated unpaid share capital liabilities among the shareholders and provided for repayment over ten years. On 13 October 2010, the second defendant, third defendant and Ms Lim signed the October Agreement, under which the second and third defendants would resign as directors that day and transfer all their shares, except for 10% retained by the third defendant, to Ms Lim, with Ms Lim agreeing to pay the second defendant’s outstanding expenses and commission in instalments and provisions stating that the second and third defendants would have no liability to APG from a compliance perspective upon settlement and would owe nothing to the company upon settlement excluding personal tax liabilities. The second defendant resigned as a director pursuant to this arrangement. After APG was placed under judicial management in March 2011, the judicial manager discovered that the second defendant had not paid for his shares and called on him for payment, and identified numerous payments made to or on behalf of the second defendant after incorporation which he considered unjustifiable due to lack of documentary support. APG alleges that it made payments to or on behalf of the second defendant including American Express credit card expenses of $326,944.34, legal expenses of $48,994.47 and A$1,987.42 in Suit No 170 of 2009 where APG was not a party, payments of $264,746.96 and A$90,191.25 without proper supporting documents, travel expenses of $33,328.37 for the second defendant and his family, payments of $245,966.83 and A$6,088.43 to third parties on his behalf, and a payment of A$86,000 from Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd into his personal bank account pursuant to an APG invoice. APG contends that these payments were for his personal use and were unauthorised. The second defendant does not appear to deny that APG’s funds were used for his personal benefit but contends that such use was authorised by APG’s directors and shareholders as part of his employment package or benefits under an alleged Oral Agreement and pursuant to an alleged Account Practice, and that APG was effectively his business and he ran it as he deemed fit. He asserts that cheque payments were authorised by directors, that payments for his personal expenses were permitted as part of his employment benefits, and that payments were made pursuant to a mode of practice for approval and authorisation of payments. He also relies on the September and October Agreements as having settled or waived APG’s claims, including unpaid share capital. He counterclaims for unpaid salary of $75,000 for five months from October 2010 to February 2011, incentives to be assessed, reimbursement of mobile phone charges, and his share of Back End Commission of $106,944 or A$83,550, and seeks an account of Back End Commission payments under the September Agreement.
Party_Details.Issue: Whether the second defendant, as a director and shareholder of APG, breached his duties by causing or permitting APG’s funds to be used for his personal benefit and by failing to pay unpaid share capital, whether the alleged Account Practice and Oral Agreement or the September and October Agreements validly authorised or settled such payments and liabilities, and whether he was entitled to his counterclaimed sums under the September Agreement.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors must not appropriate company funds for personal use and bear a prima facie burden to justify such payments as proper company expenses. They owe fiduciary duties to act in the best interests of the company and, when the company is insolvent or in a parlous financial position, to consider creditors’ interests and avoid transactions that dissipate assets to the prejudice of creditors. Contracts between a company and its directors that are entered into in breach of fiduciary duties, particularly in insolvency, are voidable at the company’s instance. Liability to pay unpaid share capital attaches to the registered holder of shares at the time a call is made, in accordance with the company’s articles on calls and forfeiture. A party asserting an authorising practice or oral agreement must substantiate it with evidence, and alleged remuneration arrangements must be consistent with other binding agreements, such as loan covenants restricting director benefits. Double recovery is not permitted where sums have already been recovered from another defendant in respect of the same payments.
Party_Details.Application: The court analysed the second defendant’s role as the director responsible for finances and his control over APG’s cheque books and bank accounts, including the change in cheque-signing authority after the non-executive directors resigned, to assess his responsibility for payments made from APG’s funds. It examined the categories of payments alleged by APG, reviewing American Express statements that showed personal expenditure such as petrol, department stores, supermarkets, restaurants and expensive clothing, and noted the absence of vouchers or records linking these expenses to APG’s business. The second defendant’s assertion that only business-related items were paid and that clothing purchases were part of his employment package was weighed against the lack of documentary evidence and the requirement that he justify such expenses as proper company expenses. For legal fees in Suit No 170 of 2009, the court considered that APG was not a party to the suit, that the second defendant received settlement monies without reimbursing APG, and that he claimed an agreement with shareholders and non-executive directors that APG would bear the fees while he kept any recovery, but produced no corroborating testimony from those directors. The court indicated that his liability for those fees should be reduced by any amount APG had already recovered from the first defendant. Payments without proper supporting documents, including those described as loans or commission, were treated as requiring explanation, and the second defendant was expected to show the purpose of each payment and his entitlement; where payments were described as loans, they were treated as sums intended to be repaid. Travel expenses for the second defendant, his family and domestic helper were examined to see if there was any evidence of contractual entitlement or business purpose, with the court noting that the only travel expense for the second defendant in the relevant appendix related to a trip to France with his wife in August 2009, for which no business connection was shown. Miscellaneous payments to third parties on his behalf, such as telephone charges, school fees and furniture rental, were assessed for any evidence that they formed part of an agreed remuneration package or were otherwise for APG’s benefit, and none was produced. The A$86,000 payment from Ubertas 350 into his personal account was scrutinised by considering his explanation that the developer required payment to him and that the funds were used exclusively for marketing events that generated sales and commission for APG. The court looked for documentary accounting of how the funds were spent and for corroboration from his assistant, whom he said collected receipts and passed them to the Accounts Department, but no such evidence or testimony was produced. The court also evaluated the second defendant’s reliance on an alleged Account Practice and Oral Agreement authorising broad reimbursement of his expenses. It looked for contemporaneous payment vouchers, testimony from the non-executive directors or staff, and consistency with the loan agreement clause prohibiting director benefits beyond ordinary salaries until Mr Goh’s loan was repaid, and with the formal, documented approach of the non-executive directors. The absence of original vouchers, the fact that Firstwaters had to reconstruct vouchers due to lack of supporting documentation, and the non-production of corroborating witnesses undermined the claimed practice and oral agreement. The court considered a letter from the non-executive directors’ solicitors denying the existence of the Oral Agreement and noted that this was not challenged by calling them as witnesses. In relation to unpaid share capital, the court traced the second defendant’s shareholdings, including his initial 360,000 shares, the acquisition of the non-executive directors’ shares increasing his holding to 640,000 shares, and the subsequent transfer of 240,000 shares to Ms Lim. It applied APG’s articles to determine that liability for calls on unpaid capital attached to the registered holder at the time of the call in March 2011, and distinguished between shares still held by the second defendant and those transferred to Ms Lim. The court also examined the terms of the September and October Agreements, including write-offs of portions of the second defendant’s debt, APG’s assumption of part of his personal debt to the non-executive directors, reallocation and postponement of unpaid share capital obligations, and clauses purporting to absolve him from liabilities to APG, and assessed these against the directors’ duties in light of APG’s insolvency or near insolvency. It concluded that the Agreements reduced APG’s assets available to creditors and were entered into in circumstances where the directors’ interests conflicted with APG’s and its creditors’ interests, rendering the Agreements voidable at APG’s instance. The second defendant’s counterclaim, based on the September Agreement for unpaid salary, incentives, phone charges and Back End Commission, was evaluated in light of the court’s view of the Agreement’s validity.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The court held the second defendant liable to pay APG $400,000 in respect of unpaid share capital on shares he continued to hold, and to repay $919,980.97 and A$184,264.10, less any amounts recovered from the first defendant, as moneys paid by or on behalf of APG for his personal use. He was ordered, jointly and severally with the third defendant, to account for all sums paid by APG into their joint account and to repay any amounts not shown to have been used for APG’s purposes. His counterclaim for salary, incentives, phone charges and Back End Commission was dismissed, and interest and costs were awarded against him.

Party_Details.Name: Wang Zijian (also known as James Wang)
Party_Details.Facts: Wang Zijian, also known as James Wang, the third defendant, worked with the second defendant at JL Property Group Pte Ltd, a company selling Australian property, before they decided in December 2008 to start their own company to sell Australian properties and resigned from JL Property. Together with the second defendant, he approached Goh Cheng Liang for a $900,000 loan as start-up capital for APG, with Mr Goh’s nominees becoming non-executive directors and shareholders. On incorporation, the third defendant was allotted 160,000 ordinary shares in APG, issued as fully paid-up although he did not pay for them. He was APG’s sales manager and one of its executive directors, while the non-executive directors acted to protect Mr Goh’s interests. After the non-executive directors resigned in November 2009 and transferred their shares to the second defendant, the third defendant remained a shareholder with 160,000 shares. On 8 December 2009, Ms Lim was appointed a director. On 6 September 2010, the third defendant, second defendant, Ms Lim and APG executed the September Agreement, which dealt with capitalisation of sums owed to Ms Lim, her injection of $150,000 for new shares, acknowledgment by the second defendant of a debt to APG with partial write-off and instalment repayment, and allocation and repayment schedule of unpaid share capital among the shareholders, including a stated liability of $160,000 for the third defendant. On 13 October 2010, the third defendant, second defendant and Ms Lim signed the October Agreement, under which the second and third defendants would resign as directors that day and transfer all their shares, except for 10% retained by the third defendant, to Ms Lim, with Ms Lim agreeing to pay the second defendant’s outstanding expenses and commission in instalments and provisions stating that the second and third defendants would have no liability to APG from a compliance perspective upon settlement and would owe nothing to the company upon settlement excluding personal tax liabilities. The third defendant resigned as a director pursuant to this arrangement. After APG was placed under judicial management in March 2011, the judicial manager discovered that the third defendant had not paid for his shares and called on him for payment. APG claims $160,000 from him as unpaid capital due on his 160,000 shares. APG also alleges that funds were regularly transferred from APG’s bank account into a joint current account at UOB Bank Ltd opened by the second and third defendants in February 2009, and that this arrangement allowed them to receive APG funds without the need for non-executive directors’ signatures on cheques. The third defendant did not personally incur the specific categories of expenses alleged to have been paid for the second defendant’s personal use, but he was a director during the period when many of the payments were made and was involved in the management of APG until around May 2010, after which he was in Malaysia and no longer involved in daily operations. He contends that any responsibility for the expenses should rest with Ms Lim and the second defendant. He relies on the September and October Agreements as having settled or waived APG’s claims, including unpaid share capital, and pleads that payments to the second defendant were authorised by APG’s directors and shareholders as part of the second defendant’s employment package or benefits under an alleged Oral Agreement and pursuant to an alleged Account Practice. He counterclaims for unpaid salary of $25,000 for five months from October 2010 to February 2011, incentives to be assessed, reimbursement of $3,000 for mobile phone charges, and his share of Back End Commission of $53,472 or A$41,775, and seeks an account of Back End Commission payments under the September Agreement.
Party_Details.Issue: Whether the third defendant, as a director and shareholder of APG, was liable to pay unpaid share capital on his shares and to account for APG funds transferred into the joint account with the second defendant, whether the September and October Agreements could validly relieve him of such liabilities, and whether he was entitled to his counterclaimed sums under the September Agreement.
Party_Details.Rule: Liability to pay unpaid share capital attaches to the registered holder of shares at the time a call is made, in accordance with the company’s articles on calls and forfeiture. Directors owe fiduciary duties to ensure that company funds are used only for proper purposes and may be required to account for company funds they receive or control, even if they did not personally spend them on their own expenses. When a company is insolvent or in a parlous financial position, directors must consider creditors’ interests and avoid transactions that reduce the asset pool available to creditors; contracts entered into in breach of such duties are voidable at the company’s instance. A counterclaim based on a contract depends on the validity and enforceability of that contract.
Party_Details.Application: The court considered that the third defendant admitted he had never paid for his 160,000 shares and that a call for unpaid capital had been made by the judicial manager after APG went into judicial management. Applying APG’s articles, the court treated him as the registered holder of those shares at the time of the call and therefore the person liable to pay the called amount. It distinguished his position from that of the second defendant, who had transferred some shares to Ms Lim before the call, and noted that there was no transfer of the third defendant’s 160,000 shares to another person before the call. The court examined the operation of the joint UOB account opened by the second and third defendants in February 2009, into which APG funds were regularly transferred, and noted that this arrangement allowed the defendants to receive APG funds without the need for non-executive directors’ signatures on cheques. Although the third defendant did not personally incur the specific personal expenses alleged in APG’s claim, the court regarded him as jointly responsible to account for APG funds that had been paid into the joint account, because he was a joint holder of that account and had the ability to use the funds. The court limited his responsibility to accounting for sums paid into the joint account, recognising that he did not control cheques drawn solely by the second defendant after cheque-signing authority changed and that APG’s claim was focused on recovery of moneys wrongfully utilised for personal use rather than general damages for failure to implement proper accounting systems. The court evaluated the September and October Agreements, including clauses purporting to absolve the second and third defendants from liabilities to APG, in light of APG’s insolvency or near insolvency and the directors’ duties to creditors. It found that the Agreements reduced APG’s assets available to creditors by writing off or rearranging debts owed by the defendants and unpaid share capital obligations, and that the directors’ interests conflicted with APG’s and its creditors’ interests when entering into these Agreements, rendering them voidable at APG’s instance. As the third defendant’s counterclaim for salary, incentives, phone charges and Back End Commission was based on the September Agreement, the court assessed his entitlement in light of its conclusion on the Agreement’s validity.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The court ordered the third defendant to pay APG $160,000 in respect of unpaid share capital on his 160,000 shares and held that he, jointly and severally with the second defendant, must account for all sums paid by APG into their joint account and repay any amounts not shown to have been used for APG’s purposes. His counterclaim for salary, incentives, phone charges and Back End Commission was dismissed, and interest and costs were awarded against him.
